body:
## Problem

This may be setup or machine specific, but the startup is almost 4 seconds due to neovim spending 3.86 seconds on `require('vim.pack')`. Here is the startuptime file [startuptime.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26383526/startuptime.txt), and here is an excerpt with the points of interest, starting at line 63 (`:63`):

```
003.972  001.127  001.123: require('vim.diagnostic')
004.698  000.052  000.052: require('vim._async')
004.888  000.189  000.189: require('vim.version')
3864.424  3860.419  3860.179: require('vim.pack')
3864.472  000.034  000.034: require('vim.fs')
3865.047  000.311  000.311: require('catppuccin')
``` 

Upon commenting out all vim.plug instances in my `init.lua`, it startsup instantly. But the addition of a single package (catppuccin) results in the full ~3.85 second additional cost to startup-times. I could not find any other issue discussing this, and it was also encountered in both versions 0.12 and 0.13. 

## Steps to reproduce

Not sure why this is happening here.

## Expected behavior

Sub-second startup times.

## System info

- Nvim version (nvim -v): `v0.13.0-dev-29 g1bcf2d7f90` neovim/neovim@1bcf2d7f90
- Vim (not Nvim) behaves the same?: Not-Applicable
- Operating system/version: Linux 5.15.0-173-generic
- Terminal name/version: ghostty 1.3.1-arch1
- $TERM environment variable: `xterm-ghostty`
- Installation: Manual build (also seen via unstable PPA and tarball pre-built binaries)

## Additional Comments

Happy to run any diagnostics that could help y'all. Not sure if this is a `vim.pack` issue or a slow filesystem issue (this shouldn't be the case as it is a pretty powerful server machine).

comment_data.body: presumably it's trying to make network requests. though `vim.pack` should show a message after X seconds, if it detects a problem.

The `nvim-pack.log` file under state does not have any messages of interest. Is there any additional logging we could enable hidden behind a flag?

I'll try to get a packet trace of it on startup, but at first glance, the git-fetches for the `pack` repos take the same as my local machine, and the ping latencies are the same.

> presumably it's trying to make network requests. though `vim.pack` should show a message after X seconds, if it detects a problem.

I don't think this can be the case since this seems to happen at the `require('vim.pack')` stage, which is very early and before processing even 'init.lua'.

> But the addition of a single package (catppuccin) results in the full ~3.85 second additional cost to startup-times.

I have several directions to speculate the problem:

- Does this happen on the config that was migrated from 'lazy.nvim'? If yes, please make sure to remove all instances of 'lazy.nvim' usage (including in '~/.local/share/nvim'). Maybe its dark magic under the hood somehow interferes here.
- Does this happen only for `catppuccin` plugin or does this happen for some other plugin. That plugin also has some dark magic under the hood (it ["compiles"](https://github.com/catppuccin/nvim#Compile) color scheme files).

> Does this happen on the config that was migrated from 'lazy.nvim'? If yes, please make sure to remove all instances of 'lazy.nvim' usage (including in '~/.local/share/nvim'). Maybe its dark magic under the hood somehow interferes here.

Nope, it's a config I did from scratch which I've used for the past few months since `vim.pack` was pushed to 0.12 dev.

> Does this happen only for catppuccin plugin or does this happen for some other plugin. That plugin also has some dark magic under the hood (it ["compiles"](https://github.com/catppuccin/nvim#Compile) color scheme files).

It happens under all plugins

You can find the config I copied from here https://github.com/alanssitis/dotfiles/blob/laptop-config/private_dot_config/nvim/init.lua

> I don't think this can be the case since this seems to happen at the require('vim.pack') stage, which is very early and before processing even 'init.lua'.

Do you happen to know if we could add additional tracing to the init process of `require('vim.pack')`? Might be something specific to this as there's no slowdown when it is not required. If time allows, I could go through the nvim source to figure out what's being called. Wanted to do a `perf` run but I couldn't figure out how to just trace the startup.

That's odd.

> If time allows, I could go through the nvim source to figure out what's being called.

The only thing that would make sense to take a long time here is [this line](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/a2d9863c2773d69f41cc9aeba402e30fea474fdd/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua#L256), as it is the only thing that is actually computed during `require('vim.pack')`. Which, I believe, was added like this only to work around some type definition diagnostic issues.

Could you please replace it with just `local git_version` (no `vim.version.parse('1')` computation), build from source, and try to see if the slowdown persist?

@echasnovski 

I think this may be an issue at a different point. I have commented out the full body of `M.add(specs, opts)` (with the changes to `local git_version` you mentioned) and the delay still persists.

I have rebuilt from source as I did not have `ninja-build` when I first built it and it still persists.

The difference in startup time probably falls within margins of negligibility but I have included the points of interest for reference:

```
004.052  000.004  000.004: require('vim.F')
004.086  001.097  001.094: require('vim.diagnostic')
004.724  000.053  000.053: require('vim._async')
3859.514  3855.394  3855.341: require('vim.pack')
3859.566  000.036  000.036: require('vim.fs')
3860.025  000.194  000.194: require('catppuccin')
```

[Not too familiar with lua nor vim] I have a few hypotheses:
- Could it be a bad/broken directory that causes nvim to take a lot longer to find `vim.pack`? Not sure how this could be the case, though, since it was able to load other `vim.*` dependencies without any issues.
- I thought this was built into nvim, but could it be due to a slow(er) lua engine?

I'll try to see if I can run into this issue with a Ubuntu 22.04 container on my laptop.

> I think this may be an issue at a different point. I have commented out the full body of `M.add(specs, opts)` (with the changes to `local git_version` you mentioned) and the delay still persists.

I am completely clueless then, sorry. My only guess here is that this is not an issue with `vim.pack` code per se, but how the module fits in the overall Lua+startup stack. Not entirely sure where to go from here, honestly. Hopefully someone more knowledgeable will have an idea.

Did we rule out all function calls in `vim.pack`? If not, then the next steps I can suggest are:

- read `:help lua-profile` and do that early in your config perhaps via `--cmd`, and see if you can get timing info to see where the biggest cost is coming from.
- alternatively, add a `nvim_echo` call in each function in `pack.lua`, manually, to narrow down the logic that is causing the delay.
    - this is a pain, sorry. we need to add some builtin features to make this easier: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38708

```
003.972  001.127  001.123: require('vim.diagnostic')
```

This is a massive red flag. `vim.diagnostic` taking over a second to load indicates a bad user env.

---

EDIT: realised this is ms

> Did we rule out all function calls in `vim.pack`?

I am not 100% sure, but based on the `--startuptime` output (big delay at `require('vim.pack')` step and not further) it looks likely.

For example, here is the relevant part of `--startuptime` for the following config:

```lua
vim.pack.add({
  'https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.ai',
})
```

```
005.985  000.227  000.227: require('vim._async')
006.309  000.321  000.321: require('vim.version')
006.790  001.889  001.340: require('vim.pack')
006.817  000.020  000.020: require('vim.fs')
006.846  000.003  000.003: require('vim.F')
007.192  002.356  000.445: sourcing /home/user/.config/nvim-pack-demo/init.lua
```

And here is the output of the artificially slowed down *execution* of `vim.pack.add()`:

```lua
vim.pack.add({
  'https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.ai',
}, { load = function() vim.loop.sleep(1000) end })
```

```
005.502  000.233  000.233: require('vim._async')
005.816  000.310  000.310: require('vim.version')
006.276  001.816  001.274: require('vim.pack')
006.305  000.022  000.022: require('vim.fs')
006.332  000.003  000.003: require('vim.F')
1006.790  1002.352  1000.511: sourcing /home/user/.config/nvim-pack-demo/init.lua
```

My interpretation is that if `vim.pack` function call causes slowdown, then it would appear in the 'init.lua' line and not directly in the `require('vim.pack')` line.

Try this:

<details>

<summary>init.lua</summary>

```lua
-- Temporary profiler for vim.pack.add().
do
  local uv = vim.uv
  local log = 'nvim-pack-add-profile.log'
  local call_nr = 0

  vim.fn.writefile({}, log)

  local function write(line)
    vim.fn.writefile({ line }, log, 'a')
  end

  local function ms(ns)
    return ns / 1e6
  end

  local function spec_names(specs)
    local out = {}
    for i, spec in ipairs(specs or {}) do
      local name
      if type(spec) == 'string' then
        name = spec:gsub('%.git$', ''):match('[^/]+$')
      elseif type(spec) == 'table' then
        name = spec.name or ((spec.src or ''):gsub('%.git$', ''):match('[^/]+$'))
      end
      out[#out + 1] = name or '?'
      if i == 5 and #specs > 5 then
        out[#out + 1] = '...'
        break
      end
    end
    return table.concat(out, ', ')
  end

  local t = uv.hrtime()
  local pack = require('vim.pack')
  write(('%8.3f ms  require("vim.pack")'):format(ms(uv.hrtime() - t)))

  local orig_add = pack.add

  pack.add = function(specs, opts)
    call_nr = call_nr + 1

    local stats = {}
    local stack = {}

    local function hook(event)
      if event ~= 'call' and event ~= 'return' and event ~= 'tail return' then
        return
      end

      local info = debug.getinfo(2, 'nS')
      if not info or type(info.source) ~= 'string' then
        return
      end
      if not info.source:match('vim[/\\]pack%.lua$') then
        return
      end

      if event == 'call' then
        stack[#stack + 1] = {
          id = ('%s:%d %s'):format(
            info.short_src or info.source,
            info.linedefined or 0,
            info.name or '<anon>'
          ),
          start = uv.hrtime(),
          child = 0,
        }
        return
      end

      local frame = stack[#stack]
      if not frame then
        return
      end
      stack[#stack] = nil

      local total = uv.hrtime() - frame.start
      local self = total - frame.child

      local stat = stats[frame.id] or { calls = 0, total = 0, self = 0 }
      stat.calls = stat.calls + 1
      stat.total = stat.total + total
      stat.self = stat.self + self
      stats[frame.id] = stat

      local parent = stack[#stack]
      if parent then
        parent.child = parent.child + total
      end
    end

    local label = ('vim.pack.add #%d (%s)'):format(call_nr, spec_names(specs))
    local start = uv.hrtime()

    debug.sethook(hook, 'cr')
    local ok, result = xpcall(function()
      return { orig_add(specs, opts) }
    end, debug.traceback)
    debug.sethook()

    write(('%8.3f ms  %s'):format(ms(uv.hrtime() - start), label))

    local rows = {}
    for id, stat in pairs(stats) do
      rows[#rows + 1] = {
        id = id,
        calls = stat.calls,
        total = stat.total,
        self = stat.self,
      }
    end

    table.sort(rows, function(a, b)
      if a.self == b.self then
        return a.total > b.total
      end
      return a.self > b.self
    end)

    for i, row in ipairs(rows) do
      if i > 20 then
        break
      end
      write(
        ('%8.3f self  %8.3f total  %4d calls  %s'):format(
          ms(row.self),
          ms(row.total),
          row.calls,
          row.id
        )
      )
    end
    write('')

    if not ok then
      error(result)
    end

    return unpack(result)
  end

  vim.pack = pack
end

vim.pack.add({
  'https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.ai',
})
-- Temporary profiler for vim.pack.add().
do
  local uv = vim.uv
  local log = 'nvim-pack-add-profile.log'
  local call_nr = 0

  vim.fn.writefile({}, log)

  local function write(line)
    vim.fn.writefile({ line }, log, 'a')
  end

  local function ms(ns)
    return ns / 1e6
  end

  local function spec_names(specs)
    local out = {}
    for i, spec in ipairs(specs or {}) do
      local name
      if type(spec) == 'string' then
        name = spec:gsub('%.git$', ''):match('[^/]+$')
      elseif type(spec) == 'table' then
        name = spec.name or ((spec.src or ''):gsub('%.git$', ''):match('[^/]+$'))
      end
      out[#out + 1] = name or '?'
      if i == 5 and #specs > 5 then
        out[#out + 1] = '...'
        break
      end
    end
    return table.concat(out, ', ')
  end

  local t = uv.hrtime()
  local pack = require('vim.pack')
  write(('%8.3f ms  require("vim.pack")'):format(ms(uv.hrtime() - t)))

  local orig_add = pack.add

  pack.add = function(specs, opts)
    call_nr = call_nr + 1

    local stats = {}
    local stack = {}

    local function hook(event)
      if event ~= 'call' and event ~= 'return' and event ~= 'tail return' then
        return
      end

      local info = debug.getinfo(2, 'nS')
      if not info or type(info.source) ~= 'string' then
        return
      end
      if not info.source:match('vim[/\\]pack%.lua$') then
        return
      end

      if event == 'call' then
        stack[#stack + 1] = {
          id = ('%s:%d %s'):format(
            info.short_src or info.source,
            info.linedefined or 0,
            info.name or '<anon>'
          ),
          start = uv.hrtime(),
          child = 0,
        }
        return
      end

      local frame = stack[#stack]
      if not frame then
        return
      end
      stack[#stack] = nil

      local total = uv.hrtime() - frame.start
      local self = total - frame.child

      local stat = stats[frame.id] or { calls = 0, total = 0, self = 0 }
      stat.calls = stat.calls + 1
      stat.total = stat.total + total
      stat.self = stat.self + self
      stats[frame.id] = stat

      local parent = stack[#stack]
      if parent then
        parent.child = parent.child + total
      end
    end

    local label = ('vim.pack.add #%d (%s)'):format(call_nr, spec_names(specs))
    local start = uv.hrtime()

    debug.sethook(hook, 'cr')
    local ok, result = xpcall(function()
      return { orig_add(specs, opts) }
    end, debug.traceback)
    debug.sethook()

    write(('%8.3f ms  %s'):format(ms(uv.hrtime() - start), label))

    local rows = {}
    for id, stat in pairs(stats) do
      rows[#rows + 1] = {
        id = id,
        calls = stat.calls,
        total = stat.total,
        self = stat.self,
      }
    end

    table.sort(rows, function(a, b)
      if a.self == b.self then
        return a.total > b.total
      end
      return a.self > b.self
    end)

    for i, row in ipairs(rows) do
      if i > 20 then
        break
      end
      write(
        ('%8.3f self  %8.3f total  %4d calls  %s'):format(
          ms(row.self),
          ms(row.total),
          row.calls,
          row.id
        )
      )
    end
    write('')

    if not ok then
      error(result)
    end

    return unpack(result)
  end

  vim.pack = pack
end

vim.pack.add({
  'https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.ai',
})
```

</details>

```bash
nvim -u init.lua
```

Then check `nvim-pack-add-profie.log`.

I believe this may be a slowdown right before calling `require('vim.pack')`.

@lewis6991 here is the profile log:
```
   0.001 ms  require("vim.pack")
   0.178 ms  vim.pack.add #2 (mini.ai)
   0.037 self     0.042 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:376 normalize_spec
   0.032 self     0.103 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:222 get_plug_dir
   0.014 self     0.014 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:417 normalize_plugs
   0.013 self     0.055 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:407 new_plug
   0.005 self     0.005 total     2 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:346 <anon>
   0.002 self     0.002 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:938 lock_read
   0.001 self     0.001 total     1 calls  ...ma/.local/share/nvim/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua:775 <anon>

   0.834 ms  vim.pack.add #1 (mini.ai)
```

That didn't help at all. Let's widen it.

Try adding this:

<details> 

```lua
local function start_profiler()
  local stats = {}
  local stack = {}

  local function hook(event)
    if event ~= 'call' and event ~= 'return' and event ~= 'tail return' then
      return
    end

    if event == 'call' then
      local info = debug.getinfo(2, 'nS')
      if not info then
        stack[#stack + 1] = false
        return
      end
      stack[#stack + 1] = {
        id = ('%s:%d %s'):format(
          info.short_src or info.source or '?',
          info.linedefined or 0,
          info.name or (info.what == 'main' and '<main>' or '<anon>')
        ),
        start = vim.uv.hrtime(),
        child = 0,
      }
      return
    end

    local frame = stack[#stack]
    if frame == nil then
      return
    end

    stack[#stack] = nil

    if frame == false then
      return
    end

    local total = vim.uv.hrtime() - frame.start
    local self = total - frame.child

    local stat = stats[frame.id] or { calls = 0, total = 0, self = 0 }
    stat.calls = stat.calls + 1
    stat.total = stat.total + total
    stat.self = stat.self + self
    stats[frame.id] = stat

    local parent = stack[#stack]
    if parent and parent ~= false then
      parent.child = parent.child + total
    end
  end

  debug.sethook(hook, 'cr')

  return function()
    debug.sethook()

    local rows = {}
    for id, stat in pairs(stats) do
      rows[#rows + 1] = {
        id = id,
        calls = stat.calls,
        total = stat.total / 1e6,
        self = stat.self / 1e6,
      }
    end

    table.sort(rows, function(a, b)
      if a.self == b.self then
        return a.total > b.total
      end
      return a.self > b.self
    end)

    local out = {}

    for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
      out[#out + 1] = ('%8.3f self  %8.3f total  %4d calls  %s'):format(
        row.self,
        row.total,
        row.calls,
        row.id
      )
    end

    vim.fn.writefile(out, 'nvim-lua-profile.log')
  end
end
```
</details>

And use as:

```lua
local stop_profiler = start_profiler()

-- code to profile

stop_profiler()
```

Thanks @lewis6991!

<details>

<summary>We have found the culprit, cpu_info </summary>

```

```
</details>

I've verified that running `vim.uv.cpu_info()` does take a while.

<details>
<summary>This is an issue with the underlying libuv implementation</summary>

The C program:
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include "uv.h"

int main() {
        printf("start\n");
        uv_cpu_info_t* cpu_infos = NULL;
        int count = 0, i;
        int ret = uv_cpu_info(&cpu_infos, &count);
        printf("end\n");
        return 0;
}
```

Running it:
```bash
$ C_INCLUDE_PATH=.local/repos/neovim/.deps/build/src/libuv/include/ \
      gcc test.c ./.local/repos/neovim/.deps/usr/lib/libuv.a  && time ./a.out
start
end

real    0m3.860s
user    0m0.039s
sys     0m0.000s
```

I think the issue is that the machine's `/proc/cpuinfo` is 5376 lines long 😛.
</details>

I very much appreciate your time helping me figure this out. I will leave this issue open for now as I figure out how to contribute a fix to libuv.

I think we might want to rename the title of the issue to help those in the future, as well as the body.

That's great news. I asked codex to speculate potential problems and `vim.uv.cpu_info()` was the first thing it said. I disregarded this since I didn't believe this could be an issue. Now we know.

We could potentially swap out `vim.uv.cpu_info()` with `vim.uv.available_parallelism()`?

> We could potentially swap out `vim.uv.cpu_info()` with `vim.uv.available_parallelism()`?

I vaguely remember asking you exactly this during initial implementation. The answer was something along the lines "it is not quite that".

Not pre-computing and caching `vim.uv.cpu_info()` at the top level would also cover most cases. It will only be this slow in cases which are not assumed to be fast.

Could we make this a vim.pack option that, when set, doesn't call this, but if not, calls `vim.uv.cpu_info()`? I think this is such an edge case that adding a top-level cache might be overkill for this.
@lewis6991 Yep, it's fast.

> Could we make this a vim.pack option that, when set, doesn't call this, but if not, calls `vim.uv.cpu_info()`?

Configurable number of threads makes sense and was proposed as a part of `vim.pack.config()` (#34772), but that is not yet approved.

> I think the main point that I remember was that we are able to use a lot more threads than the number of CPU cores we had.

Yeah and making it slower now, after the release, only due to this relatively rare edge case seems like a step backwards. Or do you propose to use `2 * vim.uv.available_parallelism()` instead of the current `2 * vim.uv.cpu_info()`? Will this be comparable?

I propose `2 * vim.uv.available_parallelism()` in a helper function instead of a variable.

On my Macbook M4 Pro these numbers are the same (12).

> I propose `2 * vim.uv.available_parallelism()` in a helper function instead of a variable.

Sure, the number makes sense then. The helper function - maybe, but it is used only twice, so might be an overkill.
Seems like there may already be an upstream fix (https://github.com/libuv/libuv/pull/5036)
`vim.uv.available_parallelism()` gives the number of logical cores (total number of threads) 192, the number of cores is half.

title: `vim.uv.cpu_info()` is slow if `/proc/cpuinfo` is large
